findings.issue: The public web OAuth handoff lets any caller choose an arbitrary external return_to while also supplying the PKCE app_challenge. Because /v1/oauth/web/init is mounted on the public router and the app applies permissive CORS, an attacker can create a handoff with their own verifier/challenge and return_to=https://attacker.example/callback, send the victim to the returned /oauth/{provider}/start URL, receive handoff_id+app_code on the attacker callback, then redeem with the known verifier. The comment says to rely on PKCE, but PKCE only protects the party that generated the challenge; here the attacker is that party unless return_to is restricted to trusted app origins.
findings.remediation: Replace the unconditional external allow with a configured allowlist of trusted frontend origins. For LAN/dev, allow only explicitly configured app origins or constrained same-host dev ports; reject all other return_to origins before creating the handoff.

findings.issue: LoginPage's OAuth init failure path clears the PKCE verifier and invitation token but leaves any existing localStorage access_token intact. Other OAuth error paths now explicitly remove access_token, and makeRequest will continue auto-attaching a stale bearer token to later API/OAuth calls after a failed login init. The regression is not covered because the login init failure test only asserts oauth_verifier cleanup.
findings.remediation: Call localStorage.removeItem('access_token') in the LoginPage catch block and seed/assert access_token removal in the existing initOAuth rejection test.
